name: Palladium Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Palladium. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Palladium oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength for efficient absorption by Palladium oxides and contaminants

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on Palladium surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Threshold fluence for effective contaminant removal from Palladium

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of surface contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation

dwellTime.description: Effective dwell time per cleaning spot for optimal results

power.description: Typical range for Nd:YAG laser cleaning of reflective metals like Pd to avoid thermal damage; starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety. Max limited by Pd's high reflectivity (~60% at 1064nm) to prevent melting (mp 1555°C). Source: IPG Photonics specs for YLR series; studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on precious metal cleaning.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal on Pd; slower for heavy oxides (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light contaminants (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance without excessive heat. Source: Industry standards from Laserax manuals; J. Cleaner Prod. (2020) case studies on metal surface prep.

pulse_width.description: Ns regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of Pd; ps (5-50 ps) minimizes HAZ due to Pd's thermal conductivity (71 W/mK), but ns sufficient. Avoid fs for industrial scale. Recommended 20 ns for low thermal impact. Source: Laser physics in Appl. Phys. A (2015) on metal ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning to control heat accumulation on reflective Pd; higher rates speed up process but risk buildup. 50 kHz recommended for uniform cleaning without overheating. Trade-off: >80 kHz increases efficiency but requires cooling. Source: Academic lit. in J. Appl. Phys. (2017); Coherent Inc. equipment guidelines.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation on Pd without substrate damage; threshold ~2-3 J/cm² for ns pulses at 1064nm due to reflectivity. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe removal of oxides/contaminants. Conservative to account for Pd's properties. Source: Ablation studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2019); uncertainty noted for exact Pd data, extrapolated from Ni/Pt analogs.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for precise cleaning on Pd; smaller for fine details (100-200 μm), larger for areas (300-500 μm). 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage in industrial prep. Source: Equipment specs from Trumpf and Adapt Laser Systems; process optimization in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2021).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy; 2 passes typical for complete Pd surface cleaning without damage. Recommended for layered contaminants in catalysis/electronics apps. Source: Industry practice in case studies from SPIE Proc. (2016); conservative for Pd's sensitivity.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage on Pd; 30-50% for large areas, 60-80% for precision. 60% recommended to avoid gaps while minimizing overexposure/heat. Source: Process studies in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2022); typical range from manufacturer guidelines like CleanLASER.
